title: Rethinking the Value of Initial Claims as a Forecasting Tool

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This November 1998 New York Fed research paper by economist Margaret M. McConnell examines whether initial unemployment insurance claims can forecast employment changes. The study finds that claims are predictive of employment during recessions but lose predictive power during economic expansions, because the inverse relationship between layoffs and hiring that exists during downturns breaks down when hiring becomes the dominant driver of employment growth.
key_points: Initial claims consistently send an accurate signal about employment during recessions but not during economic expansions
A forecasting model incorporating initial claims improves accuracy during recessions but worsens accuracy early in expansions
During recessions, layoffs and hiring are inversely related: hiring falls early in a downturn and recovers at the end
During expansions, the inverse relationship between layoffs and hiring disappears, and hiring becomes the primary driver of employment changes
Claims are a timely measure of layoffs, but employment growth depends on both layoffs and hiring
Author Margaret M. McConnell is an economist at the Federal Reserve Bank of New York
why_it_matters: This research provides insight into the structural relationship between initial unemployment claims and broader employment dynamics across business cycles. For macro forecasting, it demonstrates that labor market indicators have varying predictive power depending on the phase of the economic cycle.
macro_context: The paper analyzes the asymmetric forecasting power of initial unemployment claims across the business cycle. During recessions, claims are a reliable leading indicator because layoffs spike and correlate inversely with hiring. During expansions, hiring dominates employment growth while layoffs remain low and lose their predictive relationship with employment changes.
